Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have what you'd call an organized study routine. I'd mostly just drift through the weeks, maybe watching some videos when I felt like it or when anxiety would spike about falling behind. With 12 online courses, I spread myself thin and never really committed to a schedule. I'm more of a hands-on learner anyway, so sitting down to study in a structured way feels unnatural to me.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything, then suddenly binge-watch a bunch of lecture videos when the guilt got too heavy. Deadlines barely motivated me since I only completed about 17% of assignments anyway. It's weird because I could ace exams when I actually showed up, but the day-to-day consistency just wasn't there.
Q3: Usually it was this gnawing anxiety that would hit me, making me feel like I needed to at least see what I was missing. Sometimes the gamified tasks would pull me in because they felt less like traditional work. Other times I'd log in just to scroll through discussions and see what other people were saying without really participating much myself.
Q4: For a brief period, the gamified elements made logging in feel almost automatic, like checking a game for daily rewards. But that wore off pretty quickly when my mood dipped. The routine never really stuck because my motivation is just so low most of the time. I'd have weeks where I logged in constantly, then weeks of nothing.
Q5: The video content was genuinely useful because I could watch, pause, and rewatch things until they clicked. I spent over four hours on videos because that's how I learn best, by seeing things demonstrated. The peer interactions were okay too when I actually engaged, because hearing different perspectives helped concepts stick better than just reading.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and a lot of the discussion prompts. Reading walls of text just doesn't work for my brain, I need to be doing something or watching something. The assignments I delayed constantly, not because they weren't important, but because starting them felt like pushing a boulder uphill when I was already feeling sad and unmotivated.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it had some interactive element or if I could see immediate results. The gamified tasks were good for that because there was instant feedback. If something felt like busywork or just checking boxes, I'd tune out completely. I needed to feel like I was actually learning something practical, not just going through motions.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they didn't exist to me, honestly. If it wasn't required, my low motivation meant I just wouldn't do it. But even required ones didn't always get done, so the distinction barely mattered. The gamified optional stuff was the exception because it felt more like play than work, which tricked my brain into engaging.
Q9: Yeah, absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and videos, then study from my tablet while walking around or lying in bed. I'm kinesthetic, so I'd sometimes pace while listening to recordings or work through problems with physical flashcards I made. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer entirely.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was reviewing downloaded materials, working through practice problems on paper, or discussing concepts with friends from my extracurriculars. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. My exam scores prove I was learning, but the LMS would've shown me as basically absent during those periods.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then never open it again on the platform. I'd have it saved on my phone and review it multiple times offline. The LMS probably thought I glanced at it for five minutes when really I studied it for hours over several days.
Q12: There were stretches where the sadness just took over and everything felt pointless. My anxiety would be screaming at me to do something, but my body wouldn't move. Having 12 courses meant there was always something demanding attention, which made it easier to just shut down completely. Those were the times my attendance really tanked.
Q13: The sheer volume of courses created this constant low-level overwhelm. When fatigue hit, the LMS was the first thing I'd abandon because I could still technically pass exams without it. My extracurriculars actually helped me cope with stress, but they also competed for time. Confusion about course structure made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face what I didn't understand.
Q14: Plenty of times I'd be logged in, scrolling through videos or forums, but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd watch a whole lecture and realize I absorbed nothing because I was anxious about something else entirely. The activity was there, but meaningful engagement wasn't. It was more like going through motions to quiet the anxiety.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they mattered. Starting felt impossible when my mood was low, and my resilience only kicked in for exams where I could cram and perform. The assignments required sustained effort over time, which my motivation just couldn't support. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow until tomorrow was too late.
Q16: Deadlines created these intense bursts where I'd suddenly engage heavily, then crash afterward. But with only 17% assignment completion, clearly deadlines weren't enough to actually make me submit work. They'd spike my anxiety without improving my output. I'd log in frantically as deadlines approached, watch a bunch of stuff, then still not submit.
Q17: Feedback was complicated because I rarely submitted enough to get meaningful feedback on assignments. When I did get exam results back and saw I did well, it almost reinforced my bad habits, like why bother with assignments if I can ace tests anyway? The gamified task feedback was more immediate and kept me engaged in those specific activities.
Q18: The course structure felt overwhelming with how much content there was across all my courses. Having so many modules and resources made it easy to get lost and just disengage entirely. The gamified elements were the one structural choice that actually worked for someone like me. But overall, the structure assumed a consistency I couldn't maintain.
Q19: The gamification made engagement easier because it matched how my brain works, wanting quick feedback and feeling like progress. But the heavy reliance on reading and traditional assignments made things harder for a kinesthetic learner. The attendance expectations were impossible for me to meet given everything else going on. More hands-on activities would have helped.
Q20: That's basically me. I'd be in the LMS watching videos, clicking around, engaging with the gamified stuff, but never actually submitting assignments. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just there because anxiety won't let you leave, but you can't actually bring yourself to do the hard work of completing things.
Q21: That also describes me in a way. My exam score was over 93, and I got a Distinction, but my LMS activity was inconsistent and my assignment completion was terrible. I learned a lot offline, through downloaded materials and my own methods. The system couldn't see that I was actually absorbing the content effectively.
Q22: Logs can show patterns of when you're online and what you click on, but they completely miss the quality of that engagement. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just anxiously scrolling. They miss all offline study, emotional state, and whether someone is genuinely understanding material. Numbers without context are pretty meaningless.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. For me, engagement happens in bursts when something captures my interest or when I find a way to make it hands-on. It's not about hours logged or boxes checked. It's about whether the material is actually getting into your head and staying there.
Q24: The LMS can't show that I'm dealing with persistent sadness and anxiety that make consistent engagement nearly impossible. It can't show that I'm a kinesthetic learner doing most of my real studying while pacing around my apartment. It can't capture the disconnect between my high exam performance and low assignment completion. My story is way more complicated than any dashboard could show.
